focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Even in daylight, Saint's Basin still looked like a lie more than a place.

South of the Vale fields the wheat stubble thinned, the ground sagged, and the color changed from dead gold to a meaner gray-green that could have passed for any bad patch in a county full of them. If Dell had not gone out there with Hester and Otto Blum's map copy and cut down through the powder to black damp weeks ago, he might have walked over it the rest of his life and called it poor ground. This morning the names Hester had written down the evening before had turned into stakes driven in a rough arc, two shovels already standing in the dirt like grave markers, a pick, a spud bar borrowed from the school furnace shed, and a handful of townspeople trying not to admit they had come to see whether the basin would give up water or just one more public embarrassment.

The heat had started early. By nine the sun had a hard brass look through the dust haze and sweat was already making mud under Dell's hatband. He had his shirt stuck to his spine and the skin across his palms hot and thin from yesterday's handle work before they had even cut the first proper line. Hester stood at the edge of the marked trench with Otto's folding rule in one apron pocket and her cough tied down behind a square of faded muslin at her throat. She had hammered the stakes herself, though Dell had watched her have to stop twice to get her breath. Cora was in men's work gloves too big through the fingers, sleeves rolled high on her forearms. Thea had pinned her skirt up awkwardly for walking in ruts and tied a handkerchief over her dark hair. She looked wrong for a ditch and stubborn enough to stay in it all day.

That made nine people, if he counted the boy.

Not all of them were there for the same thing. M...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...k. Keep Dell from town talk. Protect Thea from hearing it sideways. But Dell was standing in the same sun that had just stripped Ezra bare, and she was done pretending one more evening's delay would count as protection.

"Now I tell Hester what he admitted," she said. "Then we go home and decide the rest with the door shut."

"No." The refusal came before she had finished setting it down. Dell still would not look at her. "No more shut doors."

She turned toward him. "Dell."

He jammed the red deposit book into his back pocket so hard the cover bent. "If we haul this back to the farm and sit on it till supper, by morning it's his story again." He jerked his chin toward the bank. "Hester hears it now."

"And then what?"

He looked at her at last. His face was young and mean and wrecked all at once. "Then if the basin's the thing he took first, we stop talking like it's a ghost and start digging."

"You're talking with your blood up."

"Maybe." He swallowed. "I'm still right."

She could have told him no. She could have hauled mother-authority over both of them like a quilt and smothered the afternoon under it till dark. Then August would have had the first quiet night. Hester would have heard the bank's version through somebody else's mouth. Thea might have caught it on the street before Cora could get home. Secrecy had already done enough work for the dead.

"All right," she said. "We take it to Hester now. I'll tell Thea myself after."

Dell nodded once. That was all. Then he went to the mare and caught the bridle before Cora could. He turned her east, away from the Vale road and straight down Main Street toward Hester's bakery, in full view of the bank windows. Cora took the trailing rein and went with him. Let August watch where his neat little confession was headed.
